coredata.dc:title: Recycling graphite from waste lithium-ion batteries for carbon dots@graphite (CDs@Gra) hybrid as advanced potassium ion battery anode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Zhaoxia
Xia, Peng
Liu, Xinyun
Jin, Xuanyang
Lu, Shengjun
Zhang, Yufei
Fan, Haosen
coredata.dc:description: With the explosive growth of waste lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), it is economically attractive and environmentally significant to effectively recycle the useful metals such as Li, Co or Fe from cathode materials. However, few works are reported on the recycle of waste graphite for next-generation secondary batteries. Herein, carbon dots@graphite (CDs@Gra) hybrid was successfully prepared with uniform size and high dispersibility carbon dots coated on the surface of recycling graphite by one-step carbonization process by using glucose as the carbon source. The small and quantum size of CDs can reduce the ion diffusion length and provide a continuous transport channel, endowing CDs@Gra with high ion storage capacity. When used as potassium ion battery anode material, CDs@Gra anode can reach high discharge specific capacity of 207.7 mAh g−1 and can maintain the reversible capacity of 155.8 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles at the current density of 1 A g−1, accompanying with the initial coulombic efficiency of 96.32 % for the first cycle. This method provides a high-efficiency way to prepare hybrid carbon materials for advanced potassium ion battery anode materials through recycling the waste graphite from useless lithium-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots@graphite
Anode
Potassium-ion battery
Spent lithium-ion battery
Recycling graphite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation procedure of CDs@Gra.
SEM images of (a, b, c) Graphite and (d, e, f) CDs@Gra.
TEM of (a, b, c) CDs@Gra; (d) EDS elemental mapping images of CDs@Gra.
Structural characterization of CDs@Gra. (a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) N2 ad-desorption isotherms, and (d) pore size distribution.

Electrochemical test. (a) CV curves of CDs@Gra. (b) Charge-discharge curves of CDs@Gra at different current densities. (c) Cycling performance of CDs@Gra at a current density of 0.1Ag−1. (d) Charge and second discharge profiles of CDs@Gra at a current density of 0.1Ag−1. (e) The rate capability of CDs@Gra from 0.1 to 5.0Ag−1. (f) Comparison of the rate capability of CDs@Gra anode with reported anodes for KIBs in literatures. (g) Cycle stability of two electrodes at a high rate of 1Ag−1.

Kinetic analysis of CDs@Gra for PIBs. (a) CV curves with various scan rates. (b) b values of the log (scan rate) vs. log (peak current) with various ox/red states. (c) The ratio of capacitive contribution in CDs@Gra with various scan rates. (d) The ratio of the charge contribution (shaded area) to the current at a scan rate of 1.0 mV s−1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The charge-discharge curve of CDs@Gra electrode for the first cycle. (b) Voltage response over time during a single current pulse. (c) Diffusion coefficient of K+ during the first discharge and charge. (d) Nyquist plot of CDs@Gra.

head.ce:title: Recycling graphite from waste lithium-ion batteries for carbon dots@graphite (CDs@Gra) hybrid as advanced potassium ion battery anode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jieyang Branch of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory, Guangdong University of Technology, Jieyang 515200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jieyang Branch of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jieyang Branch of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory, Guangdong University of Technology, Jieyang 515200, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Metallurgy Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Metallurgy Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Metallurgy Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the explosive growth of waste lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), it is economically attractive and environmentally significant to effectively recycle the useful metals such as Li, Co or Fe from cathode materials. However, few works are reported on the recycle of waste graphite for next-generation secondary batteries. Herein, carbon dots@graphite (CDs@Gra) hybrid was successfully prepared with uniform size and high dispersibility carbon dots coated on the surface of recycling graphite by one-step carbonization process by using glucose as the carbon source. The small and quantum size of CDs can reduce the ion diffusion length and provide a continuous transport channel, endowing CDs@Gra with high ion storage capacity. When used as potassium ion battery anode material, CDs@Gra anode can reach high discharge specific capacity of 207.7 mAh g−1 and can maintain the reversible capacity of 155.8 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles at the current density of 1 A g−1, accompanying with the initial coulombic efficiency of 96.32 % for the first cycle. This method provides a high-efficiency way to prepare hybrid carbon materials for advanced potassium ion battery anode materials through recycling the waste graphite from useless lithium-ion batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are now widely used in emerging energy storage fields such as new energy vehicles and large industrial energy storage systems. The number of retired power batteries is exponentially increasing and the recycling of various lithium-ion battery (LIBs) components has attracted global attention [1–4]. To date, the research about recycling technique mainly focuses on the waste LIBs pretreatment, the valuable metals recycling, and the reconstruction of waste cathode materials from waste LIBs [5]. However, less attention is focused on the recovery of the graphite anode materials. Considering the high consumption of graphite in LIBs and the growth of resulting waste graphite, it is of great significance to recycle anode materials to expand the supply as well as make up for the recycle of graphite. In the recycling industry of waste lithium-ion batteries, waste graphite is usually burned as slag or fuel ascribing to the lack of cost-effective refreshing technique. With the aggravation of environmental pollution, driven by green economy, it is urgent to develop a new type of recycling technology. The development of green and efficient recycling technology for waste graphite, therefore, can obtain great economic benefits and promote the environmental remediation.

As a type of anode material, waste graphite shows high irreversibility compared with clean graphite, which is mainly due to the growth of surface film and structure damage. Thus, the irreversible capacity of waste graphite must be eliminated when used as the anode material of the battery. This can be solved by coating amorphous or pyrolytic carbon materials on the surface of graphite, such as phenolic resin [6], which includes a two-stage reconstruction method of acid treatment and carbothermic reduction structure repair [7]. In recent years, some researchers have eliminated part of the irreversible capacity by loading carbon points on the surface of graphite and carbon materials, so as to speed up ion insertion/deinsertion and improve the capacity of electrode materials [8–10]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of nano-sized carbon-based materials (particle size is typically less than 10 nm), have been extensively studied and exploited because of their good biocompatibility, non-toxicity and chemical inertness [11]. Carbon dots (CDs) can create excellent platforms for intercalation between electrodes and electrolytes. Growing CDs with atomic size on waste graphite substrates can provide more active sites for ion insertion and extraction, enhance kinetics and increase capacity [12,13]. These outstanding properties have attracted significant interests in the usage of CDs for a wide range of applications, including drug delivery [14], LED [15], ion detection [16], and catalysis [17]. Recently, research interests have gradually focused on the application of CDs and other carbon materials in the field of energy storage, including capacitors and Li/Na/K ion batteries [18–21]. Jing et al. have demonstrated that a carbon quantum dot coated Mn3O4 composite (Mn3O4/Cdots) can significantly improve the ultra-high capacity and ultra-long lifetime in lithium storage [22]. Hou et al. proposed that carbon quantum dots CQDs can be transformed into 3D porous carbon frameworks, which have ultra-long sodium storage properties, ultra-long cycle life and ultra-high rate capacity [11]. To date, many efforts have been paid on the utilization of the carbon dots in secondary batteries due to their physical and chemical properties advantages. However, the construction of high-performance CDs and other carbon-based heterostructure and their efficient utilization in advanced SIBs/PIBs electrode materials are still a big challenge [23–28].

Herein, inspiring by the exceptional properties of carbon dots and the green recycling of spent LIBs graphite anode material, carbon dots confined recycling graphite hybrid (CDs@Gra) was successfully prepared by one-step carbonization of waste graphite and glucose (G@Gra) mixture. The precursor of G@Gra is prepared by the traditional chemical doping method, in which glucose molecules can coat on the surface of graphite. After pyrolysis at 200 °C for 2 h, CDs with uniform size and high dispersibility were obtained in the surface of recycling graphite (Gra). The abundant internal pores in the waste graphite offer the possibility of glucose loading. Glucose is an economical carbon source that is readily carbonized to CDs at 200 °C [29]. Due to its high solubility, glucose molecule can be embedded in the surface and cavities of the waste graphite, denoted CD@Gra, without changing the morphology of the original waste graphite. The reaction kinetics is enhanced by efficient electron and ion transfer channels due to the unique structure of CD@Gra. The experimental results show that the graphite anode material modified by CDs have significantly improved potassium-ion storage performance, including coulombic efficiency, rate capability and cycle performance. This method also presents the preparation of waste graphite based hybrids electrode materials for the potential application in other energy storage devices such as lithium/sodium ion batteries and supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovering graphite from waste lithium-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Waste lithium-ion batteries are provided by Shenzhen Commercial Battery Company, and they are separated into independent cylindrical batteries. For the safety, the battery was discharged below 2 V and manually removed by cutting. When the electrolyte enters the electrode material and forms decomposition products during the charge-discharge cycle, the opened battery is almost dry. The anode black block was separated from the copper foil by vigorously stirring in distilled water medium at room temperature at the speed of 1200 rpm for 30 min. The black lump was washed, filtered and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 8 h. Finally, the obtained sample was annealed at 300 °C under nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h to remove PVDF binder and the final product was noted as the recycled graphite (Gra) [30].

200 mg of Gra powder was immersed in a glucose (G) solution with a concentration of 5 mM, and the volume ratio of ethanol to water in the glucose solution was 9: 1. The mixture was then vigorously stirred for 6 h, centrifugation, washed, and dried in vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h. After that, G@Gra powder was uniformly distributed into a porcelain boat and carefully placed into a tube furnace, and then the sample was heated at 200 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 under nitrogen atmosphere. After cooling down, carbon dots coated graphite heterostructure (CDs@Gra) was obtained.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, JSM-7001F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JSM 2100F) were used to analyze the sample morphologies and elemental composition. A PW3040/60 X-ray diffractometer with a Cu radiation source was used to examine the crystal structure of materials. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements were conducted using an Autosorb-iQ (Quantachrome Instruments) BET analyzer, which provided the information of surface area and pore size distribution. The defects in the samples were detected by a Raman spectrometer.

The performance of the CDs@Gra for PIBs was evaluated by fabricating CR2032 coin cells (in a glovebox with O2 and H2O concentrations below 0.1 ppm). The operation was conducted at room temperature with potassium foil as anode. The electrolyte was 1.0 M of KPF6 in 1, 2-Dimethoxyethane (DME). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed with a potential range of 0.05–2.8 V and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a frequency range of 100 kHz-0.01 Hz by employing a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation. The NEWARE battery tester was utilized for the galvanostatic profile and titration method (GITT) tests.

As shown in Fig. 1 , carbon dots-confined graphite (CDs@Gra) was prepared by the one-step carbonization method using glucose as carbon sources. The precursor of G@Gra is prepared by a coating and the subsequent annealing methods, in which glucose molecules firstly can uniformly dispersed on the surface of recycle graphite. After pyrolysis process, CDs@Gra hybrids were obtained with uniformly and highly dispersed CDs on the surface of recycle graphite.

The comparison of the structure and surface morphology of CDs@Gra and Gra is shown in Fig. 2 . It can be inferred that the particle sizes of CDs@Gra and Gra are approximately in the range of 10 to 20 μm. As illustrated in Fig. 2b and c, some nanoparticles are attached to the surface of graphite particles, and they may have impurities and defect particles within the charge/discharge process. As depicted in Fig. 2e and f., After the cleaning process, the impurities on the material surface have been somewhat reduced, while the material itself remains essentially unchanged.

The morphology of CDs@Gra was further studied by transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis (Fig. 3a-c). As can be seen from the TEM image in Fig. 3c, CDs are spherical mono-disperse particles with narrow size distribution in the diameter range of 3–5 nm. Most CDs in Fig. 3b show obvious parallel planes, which proves their crystal properties. The lattice stripes with a plane spacing of 0.21 nm were observed in the HRTEM image (Inset of Fig. 3c), corresponding to the (002) plane of carbon dots. This proper interlayer spacing can provide a short diffusion length in the electrode, which can facilitate the insertion and extraction of K ions. The unparalleled electrochemical performance is due to the appropriate interlayer spacing which provides an insertion site for K+ (1.38 Å). In addition, the quantum size of CDs can reduce the ion diffusion length and provide a continuous transmission channel, leading to a high potassium storage capacity for CDs@Gra. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3c) confirms that the hexagonal crystal structure of carbon dots-coated graphite is in line with the XRD results. Fig. 3d shows the uniform distribution of C and O elements in CDs@Gra material.

The XRD pattern shows that the two samples display strong and sharp peaks at 2θ ~26°, which relates to the (002) plane of graphite. The results indicate that all samples maintain the graphite crystal structure, and the loading of carbon dots significantly reflects no change in the graphite structure. The (002) peak of CDs@Gra seems to move to the higher angle compared with Gra, which is closer to that of commercial graphite in the reference [18]. The above shows that carbon dots loading is beneficial to the recovery of graphite structure. Meanwhile, it can be observed that the (002), (101), and (004) characteristic peaks of graphite loaded with carbon dots are weakened in different degrees, which may ascribe to the size reduction and graphitization of CDs@Gra, resulting in a graphene-like structure. The Raman spectra reveals a high-strength D-band in CDs@Gra due to the surface defective functional groups of impurities decomposed by electrolytes (Fig. 4b). In addition, the additive carbon black may lead to D-band in CDs@Gra samples. However, the carbonization improved the graphitization degree after washing because most of the residual impurities on the surface of recycled graphite were removed. The ratio of D to G band intensity (ID/IG) of CDs@Gra was calculated to be 0.27 according to the peak intensities of D and G peaks, and the low ID/IG value of CDs@Gra indicated the purification of graphite carbon. In addition, D′ bands on the G-band of CDs@Gra and Gra were also observed, which ascribes to a few layered graphene and may be related to the disordered structure introduced by defects or heteroatoms [31]. The N2 ad/desorption isotherm and the corresponding pore size distribution of the product are shown in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d, respectively. It can be clearly observed that the adsorption/desorption isotherms exhibit IV types of models, representing the typical categories of mesoporous materials. There are more active sites on the surface of the waste graphite loaded with carbon dots, and carbon dots (CDs) can create an excellent interface between the electrode and the electrolyte intercalation, thus rapidly producing a uniform SEI film to improve the interface wettability and the first Coulomb efficiency of the battery [32].

The performance of carbon dots confined graphite electrodes was studied by electrochemical test and the results were shown in Fig. 5 . The initial three CV curves display the reversible and stable curves with little capacity loss, which can be attributed to the formation of irreversible solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film (Fig. 5a). Fig. 5b shows the charge-discharge curves of CDs@Gra at different current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 A g−1 from 0.05 V to 2.8 V. Fig. 5c shows the cyclic curve of CDs@Gra at 0.1 Ag−1 in which the specific capacity of CDs@Gra is stable and maintains at 207.8 mAh g−1, and the initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) reaches 96.32 %. The columbic efficiency was close to 100 % after 100 cycles. The constant current charge-discharge diagram of the first three cycles is shown in Fig. 5d, and the voltage platform of the charge-discharge curve is in line with the redox peak of the CV curve. The first loop curve is caused by the formation of SEI film and the charge-discharge curves show excellent overlap from the second cycle, which proves the extraordinary cycle stability of the composites. The rate capability test in Fig. 5e illustrates that all rates of CDs@Gra are higher than that of Gra sample. The output values are 207.7, 186.4, 174.2, 162.5, 155.9, and 142.1 mAh g−1, and the corresponding current densities are 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A g−1, respectively. In addition, the discharge capacity can be recovered to 209.1 mAh g−1 after cycling at a high rate of 5 A g−1, indicating no destruction in the structure of CDs@Gra material during high-speed cycling. The specific capacities of recycled graphite materials reported in recent years are summarized in Fig. 5f. CDs@Gra can be used as the anode material of high-performance potassium ion batteries because of its higher specific capacity and superior rate performance. Fig. 5g shows the long-term cycling of CDs@Gra and Gra materials at 1 A g−1 current density after five cycles at 0.05 A g−1. The anode material of CDs@Gra can maintain the high reversible capacity of 155.8 mAh g−1, and the capacity can almost reach 121.2 % of the initial capacity after 1000 cycles, which proves the excellent stability of CDs@Gra.

To clarify the mechanism of potassium storage at the CDs@Gra electrode, a CV test was conducted to investigate the influence of capacitance behavior on electrochemical performance at different scanning rates between 0.1 and 1.0 mV S−1. As shown in Fig. 6 , the storage of potassium ions is ruled by diffusion control behavior and pseudo capacitance behavior in the charge-discharge cycles [33,34].
(1)
i = a
v b
(2)
log
i
= b log
v
+ log
a
(3)
i
V
=
K 1
v +
K 2
v
1 / 2

In general, the relationship between the diffusion and pseudo-capacitance behavior of a battery during charging and discharging can be expressed by formulas (1) and (2). As shown in Fig. 6a, the redox peaks 1, 2, 3, and 4 in CV are selected, and the logarithm (i) and logarithm (v) are used as variables to draw a graph. By fitting the slope, the value of B is obtained, and the values of peak 1, peak 2, peak 3, and peak 4 are 0.745, 0.702, 0.786, and 0.972, respectively, which indicates that the potassium storage process of CDs@Gra is dominated by diffusion and pseudo-capacitance (Fig. 6b).

Fig. 6c presents the corresponding capacitance/diffusion contribution ratio at different scanning rates. At different scanning rates of 0.1–1.0 mV s−1, the pseudo-capacitance contribution rates are 84.91 %, 87.98 %, 90.48 %, 92.12 %, 93.07 %, and 94.36 %, respectively. The capacitance contribution gradually increases with the increase of scanning rate, suggesting a rapid potassium intercalation. 94.36 % of the total charge storage is contributed by capacitance when the scanning rate is 1.0 mV s−1 (Fig. 6d). The high pseudo-capacitance behavior verifies that more K+ are adsorbed on the electrode, which is beneficial to the rapid adsorption and separation of K+. Therefore, the CDs@Gra electrode possesses relatively high-rate capacity.

The evaluation of the diffusion coefficient of K (DK +) by constant current intermittent titration (GITT) is shown in Fig. 7a, and the calculation obeys the formula of Fick's second law [35]. Fig. 7b displays a single GITT diagram of the CDs@Gra electrode during discharge and the ΔEs and ΔEτ can be calculated [36–38].
(4)
D
K +
=
4 πτ
m
V M
MS
2
Δ
E s
Δ
E τ
2

The chemical diffusion coefficient of potassium ion in CDs@Gra electrode is displayed in Fig. 7c, which shows a slight change during discharge. However, an obvious float can be observed during charging, which decreases and rises between 2.0 V and 2.5 V. This shows that the diffusion of potassium ions in electrode materials goes through a dynamic process. In detail, the value of DK + decreases with the increase of voltage during charging. The slope capacity is provided by potassium ions adsorption on the surface of the carbon layer at the beginning of charging, which present a large specific surface area and provides more adsorption sites. In this process, potassium is easy to transfer, which is reflected in the high value of DK +. After that, the potassium diffusion process is the same in the voltage range of 0.5–2.0 V, indicating that the potassium diffusion process reaches equilibrium in this range. Finally, the value of DK + is low ascribing to the slow intercalation of potassium in the carbon layer. The value of DK + decreases with the further decrease of voltage, which indicates that the of K+ insertion into the carbon layer is more difficult, which leads to the decrease of voltage. The calculation of the ion transport coefficient illustrates a high ion transport capacity of CDs@Gra electrode. The value of the CDs@Gra electrode is relatively high, which may be due to its layered porous structure with excellent potassium ion regulation energy. The carbon dots attached into CDs@Gra improve the interface contact between materials and electrolytes, which shortens the diffusion path of potassium ions, increases large amounts of active sites, and accelerates the charge transfer. However, ions migration needs to overcome the interfacial resistance through the phase interface, leading to the reduction of ion diffusion coefficient. The result also explains why DK + decreases between 2.0 V and 2.5 V.

EIS was conducted to further evaluate the electrochemical performance of CDs@Gra electrode materials. Fig. 7d shows the impedance contrast diagram of the CDs@Gra electrode before and after cycling as the anode of the potassium ion battery. The initial CDs@Gra electrode shows a higher charge transfer resistance (Rct) value than the recycled CDs@Gra electrode. It reveals that the initial CDs@Gra electrode displays a high internal resistance due to its inferior intrinsic conductivity and lack of electron conduction path. It can be observed that the charge transfers resistance (Rct) of the CDs@Gra electrode decreases significantly with the cycle, which means that the wettability of the electrode gets better, the ion transport channel is opened, and the ion-electron transport environment is improved with the cycle.

In summary, carbon dot coating recycling graphite hybrid (CDs@Gra) was prepared by the one-step carbonization method. The precursor of G@Gra was prepared by the doping method, in which glucose can heterogeneously surround the Gra to form thin layer. After pyrolysis process, CDs with uniform size and high dispersibility were obtained on the surface of recycling graphite. The experimental results demonstrate that the CDs@Gra electrode can provide the specific capacity of potassium ion discharge of 207.7 mAh g−1 after charging the potassium battery as the anode material. Moreover, the reversible capacity of 155.8 mAh g−1 can be maintained after 1000 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, and the coulombic efficiency of the first cycle can reach 96.32 %. This work brings a novel research method for the recycling of waste lithium batteries in the future by using green and simple methods to recycle waste graphite anode as the anode of potassium ion batteries.

Zhaoxia Qin: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Peng Xia: Data curation. Xinyun Liu: Resources. Xuanyang Jin: Software. Shengjun Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yufei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Haosen Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52472194, 52101243), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (2025A1515012571; 2023A1515012619) and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (202201010565). We would like to thank Analysis and Test Center of Guangzhou University for their technical support.
